talk_title: Colibactin-producing pks+ E. coli adhesion drives CRC in mouse models

speaker.name: Lars Verbeke
speaker.affiliation: Ghent University and VIB, Flemish Institute for Biotechnology

targets.dependency_context: pks+ E. coli colonizing intestinal epithelium

biomarkers.name: γ-H2AX
biomarkers.finding: Increased γ-H2AX-positive cells in colon tissue of mice colonized with wild‑type 11G5 but not with clbQ‑deficient strain

models.name: Zap2 (ZEB2) intestinal epithelial transgenic
models.genotype_or_key_features: ZEB2 overexpression in IECs, germ‑free or colonized with 11G5 or Nissle 1917

MOA_summary: pks+ E. coli adheres to intestinal epithelium via high‑affinity FimH/FimLH adhesins, positioning the unstable colibactin toxin in close proximity to host DNA where it cross‑links AT‑rich sequences, inducing a SBS88/ID18 mutational signature and driving colorectal tumorigenesis; pharmacologic blockade of FimH (Sibofimlock) reduces bacterial adhesion, colibactin‑induced DNA damage, and tumor development in germ‑free‑derived Zap2 mice.
key_findings: Only mice colonized with the CRC isolate 11G5 (pks+, clbQ+) showed severe CRC phenotypes, whereas Nissle 1917 did not despite both being pks+.
Deletion of clbQ abolished the tumor‑promoting effect of 11G5, confirming colibactin dependence.
FimH or FimLH loss in 11G5 eliminated epithelial binding, γ‑H2AX induction, and CRC exacerbation.
High‑affinity 11G5 FimH variants restored binding and genotoxicity when expressed in Nissle, demonstrating variant‑specific adhesion strength.
Sibofimlock, a small‑molecule FimH inhibitor, dose‑dependently reduced bacterial adhesion and colibactin‑driven tumor development in vivo.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: E. coli Nissle 1917, although pks+, fails to promote CRC due to poor epithelial adhesion, highlighting adhesion as a selectivity determinant.

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit bacterial adhesion to prevent colibactin delivery to host DNA
combination_strategies.phenotype: Dose‑dependent reduction in epithelial binding and CRC development

open_questions: What cellular receptor or transport mechanism mediates colibactin entry into the nucleus after epithelial adhesion?
Do other Enterobacteriaceae employ similar adhesin variants to modulate colibactin toxicity?

talk_title: Sulfur amino‑acid diet, Mucispirillum schaedleri and NKT‑XCL1‑CDC1 axis in colorectal cancer

speaker.name: Wendy Garrett
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Chan School of Public Health

targets.dependency_context: High sulfur amino‑acid diet in mice
NKT cell production in response to M. schaedleri phospholipids
Activated by XCL1 in high‑diet mice

biomarkers.name: CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: Increased CD8⁺ T cells and granzyme B in tumors of mice on high sulfur amino‑acid diet

biomarkers.name: XCL1 levels
biomarkers.finding: Elevated XCL1 produced by NKT cells; neutralization abolishes diet‑induced anti‑tumor effect

models.name: Cdx2‑Cre;Apc^fl/fl (ApcMin) intestinal tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Conditional Apc loss in colon epithelium; used with high vs low sulfur amino‑acid diets

models.name: MC38 flank tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mismatch‑repair‑deficient colon carcinoma; used to test diet effects

models.name: Apc/Kras/Trp53 organoids (orthotopic injection)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Orthotopic colon tumor model for diet studies

models.name: Peripheral blood mononuclear cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: NKT cell activation by M. schaedleri conditioned media

MOA_summary: A high sulfur‑amino‑acid diet thickens the colonic inner mucus layer, creating a niche for Mucispirillum schaedleri; this bacterium releases phospholipids that activate NKT cells to secrete XCL1, which in turn recruits and activates Batf3‑dependent CDC1 dendritic cells, enhancing CD8⁺ T‑cell cytotoxicity and reducing adenocarcinoma burden in Apc‑driven mouse models.
readouts: flow cytometry for CD8⁺ T cells and granzyme B
key_findings: High sulfur amino‑acid diet increased inner mucus thickness without increasing goblet cell numbers.
M. schaedleri bloomed in high‑diet mice across three tumor models (MC38, ApcMin, orthotopic organoids).
Diet‑induced tumor protection was lost in Batf3‑KO mice lacking CDC1 dendritic cells.
Neutralizing XCL1 abolished the anti‑tumor benefit of the high diet, implicating NKT‑derived XCL1 as essential.
Conditioned media from M. schaedleri stimulated NKT cells to produce XCL1 in an MR1‑independent manner.
open_questions: What are the specific phospholipid species from M. schaedleri that trigger NKT cell activation?
Can dietary modulation of sulfur amino acids be safely translated to human CRC prevention or therapy?

talk_title: Intratumoral Fusobacterium nucleatum drives epithelial quiescence and therapy resistance

speaker.name: Susan Bowman
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: Enriched in CRC tumors, especially clade 2 (F. animalis)
Up‑regulated in epithelial cells upon Fusobacterium exposure

biomarkers.name: Ki‑67
biomarkers.finding: Reduced Ki‑67 staining in high‑Fusobacterium density tumor regions

biomarkers.name: Cell‑cycle phase (G0/G1 arrest)
biomarkers.finding: Dose‑dependent increase in G0/G1 fraction of CRC spheroids at MOI ≥10

biomarkers.name: 5‑FU pathological response
biomarkers.finding: Patients with high Fusobacterium burden showed lower major pathological response to neoadjuvant 5‑FU

models.name: CRC resection specimens
models.genotype_or_key_features: Spatial mapping of Fusobacterium by RNA‑scope and 10x Visium

models.name: CRC cancer cell spheroids
models.genotype_or_key_features: Co‑culture with clinical Fusobacterium isolate; neutrophil recruitment assays

models.name: Fusobacterium‑colonized ApcMin mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Oral gavage of F. nucleatum; tumor multiplicity assessment

models.name: InvadeSeq / COSMIC spatial transcriptomics
models.genotype_or_key_features: Single‑cell resolution of bacterial‑infected vs uninfected niches

MOA_summary: Intratumoral Fusobacterium nucleatum localizes predominantly extracellularly, where its phospholipid components trigger epithelial MAPK activation and CXCL8 secretion, leading to a dose‑dependent G0/G1 cell‑cycle arrest (quiescence) without increasing proliferation; this quiescent state reduces 5‑FU efficacy and creates an immunosuppressive microenvironment characterized by myeloid infiltration and checkpoint up‑regulation.
readouts: tumor weight and number in mice
key_findings: Fusobacterium signals are mainly extracellular; bacterial load correlates with increased inter‑cellular spacing in tumor tissue.
High bacterial density regions show reduced Ki‑67 and a shift toward G0/G1 arrest in cancer cells (MOI ≥10).
In vitro, Fusobacterium exposure induces MAPK (p‑p38, FOSL) activation and CXCL8 secretion in a dose‑dependent manner.
Patients with high intratumoral Fusobacterium have lower major pathological response to neoadjuvant 5‑FU therapy.
Clade 2 (F. animalis sensu stricto) dominates CRC tumors and possesses acid‑resistance (GAD) and ethanolamine utilization genes, suggesting selective fitness.
Spatial profiling reveals Fusobacterium‑rich niches enriched for myeloid cells (ARG1, CD66b) and immune checkpoints (PD‑1, CTLA‑4, LAG‑3).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Quiescent G0/G1 state confers resistance to S‑phase‑targeting chemotherapies such as 5‑FU.
Extracellular bacterial load disrupts epithelial cell‑cell adhesion (loss of E‑cadherin, EpCAM) creating a protected niche.

combination_strategies.rationale: Antibiotic targeting anaerobic Fusobacterium to reduce bacterial burden and restore chemotherapy sensitivity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Ongoing clinical trial (MD Anderson) evaluating metronidazole plus standard neoadjuvant therapy

open_questions: What specific bacterial metabolites mediate MAPK activation and cell‑cycle arrest?
Does intracellular versus extracellular Fusobacterium have distinct effects on tumor immunity?
Can targeting the quiescent niche reverse 5‑FU resistance in patients?
